title: Dog Mange: Managing Crusts on Face and Ears
meta_desc: Is your dog showing crusty skin on their face or ears? Learn about mange types, urgency levels, and why professional triage is essential for relief.
content_html:
What is Mange?

Mange is a skin disease caused by tiny parasitic mites. When it affects the face and ears, it often appears as thick, greyish-yellow crusts, intense redness, and hair loss. The two most common types are Sarcoptic mange, which is highly contagious and extremely itchy, and Demodectic mange, which often targets dogs with weakened immune systems. Both conditions cause significant irritation and can lead to painful skin thickening if not addressed.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for mange is Medium. While mange itself is rarely a sudden life-threatening emergency, it causes extreme physical distress and intense itching. Without prompt treatment, your dog may develop severe secondary bacterial or yeast infections from scratching. You should aim to see a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours to start a medicated treatment plan and provide much-needed relief to your pet.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of the affected areas on the face and ears is a vital part of the triage process. Visuals allow a professional to assess the distribution of the crusts and the level of inflammation. This helps differentiate mange from other skin issues like severe allergies or autoimmune conditions, ensuring that the veterinary team can prioritize your dog's care appropriately and prepare the necessary diagnostic tools, such as skin scrapings, before you arrive.

faqs.question: Can humans catch mange from dogs?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sarcoptic mange (scabies) is zoonotic, meaning it can temporarily transfer to humans and cause itchy, red skin lesions.

faqs.question: Will over-the-counter flea medicine kill mange mites?
faqs.answer: While some modern prescription flea preventatives also treat mites, many standard over-the-counter options are not effective against mange. A vet must prescribe specific parasiticides.

faqs.question: How do vets test for mange?
faqs.answer: The most common method is a skin scraping, where a veterinarian gently scrapes the surface of the crusty area to identify mites or eggs under a microscope.
